Q1: I tried to keep a fairly consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after my extracurricular activities. I'd block out about two to three hours most days, and I found that listening to recorded lectures or audio explanations worked best for me. I kept a simple schedule on my phone to track what needed to be done each week. Even when my motivation was low, I pushed myself to stick to the routine because I knew falling behind would just make my anxiety worse.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, though I won't lie—deadlines definitely intensified things. I tried to spread my 16 hours across the week evenly, but when assignments were due, I'd find myself adding extra sessions. The stress of upcoming deadlines made it hard to relax, so I'd rather work ahead than deal with that anxious feeling of being unprepared. My attendance was nearly perfect because showing up consistently helped me feel more in control.
Q3: Honestly, I logged in mainly when I knew there was something specific I needed—an assignment to submit, a grade to check, or a discussion I needed to participate in. Sometimes I'd log in just to ease my anxiety, to make sure I hadn't missed anything important. The gamified tasks they introduced actually motivated me a bit more to check in, even if just to see my progress. But I wasn't someone who browsed around the LMS casually.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS became part of my evening wind-down routine. I'd log in after dinner, watch the required videos, and check discussion boards. The gamified elements helped make it feel less like a chore—seeing progress bars fill up gave me a small sense of accomplishment. It wasn't exciting, but it became automatic, like brushing my teeth before bed.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I learn best by listening. I'd often replay sections multiple times to really absorb the material. The discussion boards were also helpful because reading how other students understood concepts gave me different perspectives. I spent over 200 minutes on videos, and that time felt genuinely productive for my learning style.
Q6: I mostly ignored the supplementary reading materials and some of the optional PDFs. Reading dense text on a screen just doesn't stick for me the way audio does. I'd skim them if I thought they'd be on an exam, but otherwise, I focused my limited energy on what I knew would help me most. With my anxiety levels, I had to be strategic about where I put my effort.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to my grades or my understanding of core concepts. The gamified tasks were surprisingly engaging because they broke things into smaller, achievable goals. When I could see immediate feedback or progress, it felt like my effort was actually going somewhere. Vague or open-ended tasks stressed me out more than they helped.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a trap to me, honestly. I'd worry that maybe they weren't really optional, or that I'd miss something important by skipping them. Required activities had clear expectations, which actually reduced my anxiety even though they added to my workload. With optional stuff, I'd stress about whether to do them, which sometimes took more mental energy than just doing them would have.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio files and lecture recordings to listen to while commuting or doing chores. I also had a notebook where I'd write down key points while listening, which helped me process information better. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with friends from my extracurricular activities who were in similar courses. None of that showed up in the LMS, but it was a huge part of how I actually learned.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd have my notes spread out, listening to downloaded lectures on repeat, working through problems on paper. The LMS would show I'd watched a video once, but I'd actually listened to it three or four times offline. I also spent time just thinking through concepts during walks or before sleep—that mental processing was real studying, but completely invisible to any tracking system.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd stream a video once to make sure it downloaded properly, then I'd access it offline repeatedly. The same with any audio materials—I'd grab them once and then use them extensively without being connected. It saved my data and let me study in places without good internet. My actual engagement with those materials was way higher than my login frequency would suggest.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt overwhelming. My anxiety spiked, and even though I was still attending and submitting work, I felt disconnected from the material. The stress made it hard to concentrate, and I'd find myself reading the same paragraph over and over without absorbing anything. My extracurricular commitments also peaked during that time, which stretched me thin emotionally and mentally.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just go through the motions—clicking through materials without really engaging. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars sometimes meant I had to rush through course content. The medium stress level I carry made it hard to focus deeply, so I'd prioritize completing tasks over truly understanding them during those periods.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered to other worries—upcoming deadlines, personal stuff, anxiety about my exam performance. I'd technically complete the activity, but I wasn't really there mentally. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was checking boxes rather than learning. My task completion rate looks good, but it doesn't capture those empty engagements.
Q15: I postponed the more challenging problem sets sometimes, even knowing they were important for the exam. The anxiety of potentially failing at them made me avoid starting. I'd tell myself I'd do them when I felt more prepared, but that moment never really came. My resilience usually kicked in eventually and I'd power through, but that initial avoidance cost me valuable practice time.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my motivator. They created intense anxiety spikes, but they also forced me to actually complete work I might otherwise have put off. I'd usually start working on assignments well before the deadline, but the quality of my work improved as the deadline approached because the pressure helped me focus. The LMS would show me submitting early, but not the anxious energy behind that timing.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. Critical feedback, even when constructive, would send my anxiety through the roof and make me hesitant to attempt similar tasks. I appreciated detailed feedback, but I needed time to process it emotionally before I could use it productively. Sometimes I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The structured nature of the course actually helped me a lot. Having clear weekly expectations and deadlines gave me a framework to organize my studying around. The gamified elements added some variety that broke up the monotony. However, when multiple intensive weeks stacked up, the structure felt suffocating rather than supportive. I needed that predictability, but I also needed breathing room.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they turned studying into smaller, achievable challenges rather than one overwhelming mountain. The discussion board requirements pushed me to interact with peers, which I might not have done otherwise. What made things harder was when instructions were unclear or when there were too many different platforms to check. Simplicity and clarity reduced my anxiety significantly.
Q20: I think those students might be like me on my worst days—logging in, clicking around, trying to engage but not actually producing anything meaningful. High anxiety can make you feel like you need to be doing something, so you're constantly checking the LMS, but the stress blocks you from actually completing work. It could also be students who are struggling to understand the material and keep reviewing without making progress.
Q21: That definitely reflects part of my experience. My login frequency was low—just once typically—but I'd download materials and study extensively offline. Someone looking at my logs might think I was barely engaged, but I was actually putting in serious hours with audio lectures and handwritten notes. The LMS only captures online activity, not the real learning that happens away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show submission patterns, deadline compliance, and basic resource access. They're good at tracking what you clicked and when. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone was actually thinking deeply or just going through motions. They miss offline studying, emotional state, and the difference between productive struggle and anxious avoidance. Numbers don't capture understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing requirements. It's when I'm genuinely curious about a concept and want to explore it further, when I'm connecting new information to things I already know. For me, true engagement feels like active listening and thinking, not passive consumption. It's mental presence, which is something I struggle to maintain when my anxiety is high but deeply value when I achieve it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on audio learning and offline study, which was the core of my actual engagement. It wouldn't capture the anxiety that colored every interaction with course materials, or the resilience it took to keep showing up despite feeling overwhelmed. It wouldn't show the conversations with friends that helped concepts click, or the mental rehearsal I did while walking. The emotional labor of being a student is invisible to any tracking system.
